es.text: Al que madruga, Dios le ayuda
es.literal: God helps the one who rises early
es.example: Levántate temprano: al que madruga, Dios le ayuda.
es.exampleTranslation: Get up early — the early bird catches the worm.

it.text: Il mattino ha l'oro in bocca
it.literal: the morning has gold in its mouth
it.meaning: The morning hours are the most valuable.
it.example: Sveglia presto: il mattino ha l'oro in bocca.
it.exampleTranslation: Up early — the morning is worth the most.

bridge.note: Both reward the early riser, and each names a different paymaster. Spanish sends God: rise early and heaven lends a hand — the reward is grace, and it is granted to a person. Italian sends the market: the morning itself holds gold in its mouth — the reward is value, and it belongs to the hour, not to you. Divine favour against commercial worth.

interference.es_to_it: There is no God in the Italian proverb; invoking one ('Dio aiuta chi si alza presto') is understood but not the fixed form.
interference.it_to_es: The Spanish proverb has no gold and no personified morning — the helper is explicitly God.

review.notes: From Joe's proverb list 2026-07-18 (discovered via learner sites; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED, reviewer to confirm the pairing exists and is current. Divine-vs-commercial framing is the payload.
